data.title: Tech Voices: Google, Anthropic on China AI; Palantir on AI jobs impact
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis said China remains about six months behind the West in the development of AI.

“They’re very good at kind of catching up to where the frontier is and increasingly capable of that. But I think they’ve yet to show they can innovate beyond the frontier," Hassabis told Bloomberg during an interview at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. 

  • Anthropic (ANTHRO) CEO Dario Amodei said the U.S. government's decision to allow AI chips to be shipped to China carries "incredible national security implications."

“It would be a big mistake to ship these chips,” Amodei told Bloomberg in Davos. “I think this is crazy. It’s a bit like selling nuclear weapons to North Korea.”

Amodei's remarks come in the wake of U.S. approval of the sale of Nvidia (NVDA) H200 AI chips to customers in China. 

  • Palantir (PLTR) CEO Alex Karp said AI will eliminate the need for workers to migrate en masse for work, especially if they have vocational skills.

“There will be more than enough jobs for the citizens of your nation, especially those with vocational training,” Karp told Bloomberg in Davos. “I do think these trends really do make it hard to imagine why we should have large-scale immigration unless you have a very specialized skill.”

Karp added that AI will make vocational workers more valuable "if not irreplaceable," while “elite” white-collar workers would be more vulnerable to job disruptions.

data.title: Nvidia, Broadcom, NXP, Analog Devices are Citi's top picks going into earnings
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), and Analog Devices (ADI) are Citi's top picks in the semiconductor space, going into earnings season. 

“We expect a good semis earnings season and expect Street estimates to go up across most of the coverage universe,” analyst Atif Malik wrote in a note to clients. “We like data center semis the most on AI and general-purpose server demand, followed by analog semis given low inventory and depressed margins. We are cautious on PC/smartphone exposed semis on higher [bill of materials] costs impacting unit growth negatively. Our top buy-rated picks are AVGO, NVDA, ADI, and NXPI.”

Delving deeper, Malik said that Broadcom and Nvidia offer “attractive entry points,” as AI-related chip stocks have pulled back recently, amid fears related to OpenAI (OPENAI) and Oracle (ORCL). “Fundamentally, compute and networking demand for next gen reasoning models is accelerating and will likely drive upside for both AVGO and NVDA which we see as core AI holdings,” Malik added. “Nvidia at CES talked about upside to data center demand and TSMC bumped up its 2026 sales/capex growth last week.”

Additionally, Malik said the upturn in the analog market appears to be getting closer and closer, but it's unsure how long the upturn will last. As such, Analog Devices and NXP are considered the two most attractive in the space, Malik added, citing their respective valuations.

Lastly, Malik said he expects Qualcomm (QCOM) to move lower after it reports, due in part to concerns about the loss of its modem business with Apple (AAPL), while NXP Semiconductors is expected to move higher.

data.title: Qualcomm heading towards seventh straight session of losses
data.description:
Qualcomm (QCOM) was on track to widen its losses to the seventh session as the stock declined 2.73% to $155.07 during Tuesday’s mid-day trading.

The company began its downward course on January 9 when it ended 2.25% lower after being downgraded at Mizuho to Neutral from Outperform. The brokerage also cut its price target for QCOM to $175 from $200, noting it has “handset headwinds,” as it is expected to lose share in Apple (AAPL) devices this year and next. The company continued to decline in the following session, on November 12, when it lost 4.79%.

In the same week, KeyBanc also flagged that the ongoing memory supply crunch due to insatiable data center demand will likely create risks across the PC, smartphone, and automotive markets in 2026. PC unit shipments are expected to decline from 5% to 10% throughout the year, and smartphone units to decline by 3% to 5%, it added.

Seeking Alpha analyst Deep Value Investing has downgraded the stock to Sell from Strong Buy over similar fears.

“I have strong reasons to believe that the market is now more concerned with a growth stagnation in the handset segment than the upside that the new data center segment could bring to the top line of the company as soon as 2027,” the analyst said.

However, another analyst, Stone Fox Capital, has rated QCOM as a Strong Buy, arguing that the firm is “rapidly capitalizing on the surge in personal AI and robotics, reversing perceptions of missing the AI race.”

Overall, Seeking Alpha, as well as Wall Street analysts, have issued a buy call for the stock.

As per Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, the stock has a Hold rating with a score of 3.12 out of 5. QCOM has been rated a D+ for growth, but it has been graded an A+ for profitability.

data.title: Nvidia backs AI inference startup Baseten in latest funding round: report
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) has backed Baseten, a startup focused on providing inference for artificial intelligence applications, in its latest funding round, according to the Wall Street Journal.

The funding round generated $300M and has more than doubled Baseten's valuation to $5B, the report said. The round was led by IVP, a technology-focused venture capital firm, and CapitalG, which is Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) independent growth fund.

Baseten recently completed a Series D funding round of $150M in September. 

"The world's most dynamic AI companies like Abridge, OpenEvidence, Clay, Mirage, Zed, Gamma, Sourcegraph, Writer, and Bland use Baseten to power applications that reach hundreds of millions of users," said Baseten CEO and co-founder Tuhin Srivastava. "We are the best infrastructure partner to these teams—we let them take performance and reliability for granted, so they can focus on what makes them unique. As model-driven products become ubiquitous, we will be the invisible infrastructure behind the AI-first economy."

Nebius (NBIS) recently partnered with Baseten to offer AI companies and enterprises text-to-video inferencing across all its cloud regions in the U.S., Finland and France.

"Nebius provides the AI cloud foundation with dedicated GPU clusters and elastic scale, and Baseten layers on an inference stack that makes that power predictable and efficient for day-to-day workloads," Nebius said of the partnership.

Baseten also provides support for Nvidia's Nemotron 3 Nano, which is a high-accuracy, high-efficiency open AI model.

data.title: Mastercard said to be considering strategic investment in Zerohash after deal talks end
data.description:
Mastercard (MA) is now considering a strategic investment in the blockchain infrastructure company Zerohash, CoinDesk reported, citing people familiar with the transaction.

In October last year, the payments company was said to be in late-stage talks to acquire Zerohash for $1.5B-$2.0B.

Deal talks had collapsed after Zerohash opted to remain independent, a person familiar with the matter reportedly said.

Mastercard is said to be linked to a number of other potential takeover targets in the digital assets sector, including an acquisition of the stablecoin startup BVNK, the January 20 CoinDesk report noted.

data.title: Palantir's deal with HD Hyundai worth 'hundreds of millions,' report says
data.description:
• Palantir Technologies's (PLTR) deal with Korean company HD Hyundai is believed to be worth “hundreds of millions” of dollars, Reuters reported.
  • HD Hyundai, which operates in heavy industries such as shipbuilding, heavy equipment, machinery, and more, will use Palantir's software over numerous years, the news outlet added, citing sources familiar with the matter. Palantir began working with HD Hyundai in 2021. Since then, the South Korean company said it is able to manufacture ships at a 30% faster rate using Palantir's software.
  • In an interview with Reuters at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Palantir CEO Alex Karp said he was “very bullish” on Korea. He also added the country is “one of the more innovative, interesting, artistic places in the world.”
  • The companies announced the deal earlier on Tuesday, with Karp adding the company was “committed to advancing our strategic collaboration and driving the group’s competitiveness, and we look forward to continue building together.”
  • Palantir shares fell 1% in late afternoon trading on Tuesday.

data.title: Fastenal signals double-digit net sales growth in 2026 with expanding digital footprint
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fastenal Company (FAST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jeffery Watts, President & Chief Sales Officer, highlighted "Fastenal delivered a strong fourth quarter, capping an impressive 2025 recovery. We achieved double-digit growth in Q4 with daily sales up just over 11%, and we continue to gain market share despite a sluggish industrial economy." He emphasized continued success in signing national and global contracts, growing key account relationships, and a deliberate shift toward large, strategic customers. Watts reported that "our total contract customer count grew by 241 or just over 7%, reflecting solid new customer signings and expansions." He also cited a significant increase in value-added services, noting that "nearly half of our Q4 sales were transacted through FMI Technology or other digital channels."

  • Watts stated, "We grew net sales to $2.03 billion in the quarter, an increase of 11% from Q4 of last year." Net income increased 12.2% year-over-year to $294.1 million, with earnings per share of $0.26. For the full year, Fastenal achieved record annual sales of $8.2 billion, up close to 9%, and net income of $1.26 billion, up 9.4%.

  • Max Tunnicliff, Senior EVP & CFO, reported, "Operating cash flow was approximately $370 million, representing 125% of net income." He stated, "SG&A as a percentage of sales declined to 25.4% in Q4 from 25.9% a year ago." Tunnicliff also confirmed, "Operating margin benefited from disciplined SG&A management. We generated strong cash flow with capital allocation focused on growth, technology and shareholder returns."

  • Daniel Florness, CEO & Director, announced, "Congratulations to Jeff on being named our next CEO, taking effect in July. Max, who you just heard from, congrats on being named Fastenal's CFO and welcome to the Blue Team."

Outlook

  • Daniel Florness commented, "We anticipate double-digit net sales growth in 2026, supported by FMI Technology and digital solutions and feel really good about the momentum."

  • Watts and Tunnicliff both described ongoing investments in technology and FMI hardware as drivers for growth, with capital expenditures for 2026 expected to be approximately 3.5% of net sales.

  • Tunnicliff stated, "The modest annual gross margin contraction that we've seen historically should be considered in thinking about our 2026 performance. However, we believe this modest contraction will be offset within SG&A as we find efficiencies and further leverage our fixed costs."

Financial Results

  • Q4 net sales were $2.03 billion, an increase of 11% year-over-year.

  • Q4 net income was $294.1 million, a 12.2% increase from the prior year. Earnings per share were $0.26.

  • Operating cash flow for the quarter was approximately $370 million, with a full-year total of $8.2 billion in sales and $1.26 billion in net income.

  • SG&A expenses as a percentage of net sales decreased to 25.4% in Q4 2025 from 25.9% in Q4 2024.

  • Gross margin decreased 50 basis points in Q4 due to timing factors in cost of goods sold and supplier rebates.

  • The installed base of active FMI devices grew 7.6% year-over-year to about 136,600 units, and digital footprint channels represented 62.1% of Q4 sales.

Q&A

  • David Manthey, Robert W. Baird & Co., asked about double-digit net sales growth in 2026. Florness replied, "It's not guidance, Dave. It is -- we look at momentum, and that's what -- that's how we're thinking about the year."

  • Manthey inquired about rebate timing factors and their impact. Florness explained, "Depending on growth, you -- there is rebate programs that different suppliers have, and you estimate that as you go through the year. And in all honesty, it came in a little bit lower than we were expecting."

  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair, questioned incremental margins for 2026. Florness responded, "That's not an unreasonable number. Once we get -- first quarter, we still have the anniversary-ing of our bonus ramp-up."

  • Thomas Moll, Stephens, asked about stabilization in large heavy-manufacturing markets. Watts answered, "No, we're not seeing any real decline. It's really flat."

  • Kenneth Newman, KeyBanc Capital Markets, asked if the company gave up price for volume growth. Florness said, "Philosophically, we love to grow first and foremost. And we love to figure things out."

  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley, questioned price expectations in 2026. Tunnicliff said, "We will have some carryover pricing impacts. And yes, will that be substantial? No, and then we can also just say that -- and Dan alluded to this already, we will continue to go after pricing."

  • Stephen Volkmann, Jefferies, asked about e-business trends and impact on margins. Watts said, "We put a lot of time and resource into relaunching our website... we definitely see that to be becoming an increasing number as we move forward." Florness noted, "It's still a relatively small piece that it doesn't really have that much ability to move gross profit up or down."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, probing for clarity on incremental margins, pricing, macro conditions, and digital expansion.

  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing momentum and operational discipline. During Q&A, responses were measured and occasionally hesitant, particularly around pricing specifics, as seen when Florness said, "I'm not going to get too deep into the pricing piece, Dave, because we've done such an excellent job of being wrong every time we talk about it in 2025."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more optimistic about 2026 growth prospects, while analysts pressed for specifics on pricing and margin assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw explicit emphasis on double-digit net sales growth expectations for 2026, compared to a more cautious outlook in Q3.

  • Management reported continued strong key account and digital channel expansion, with a higher share of sales through FMI technology and digital platforms this quarter.

  • Gross margin contracted by 50 basis points in Q4, a reversal from the 40 basis point year-over-year increase reported in Q3.

  • SG&A as a percentage of sales declined in Q4, whereas Q3 saw employee-related expense increases due to bonus resets.

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on pricing actions, margin outlook, and macro conditions, but the tone shifted from concern over margin compression in Q3 to greater focus on growth and digital integration in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing challenges related to industrial production headwinds, timing of supplier rebates, and gross margin contraction due to cost of goods sold timing factors.

  • Florness highlighted difficulty in managing non-fastener supplier costs, stating, "With the non-fasteners, there's a piece where we have that same kind of visibility. However, there's a big piece where we don't."

  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of incremental margins, pricing fatigue, and the impact of holiday shutdowns on sequential sales patterns.

  • Tunnicliff described the macro environment as "unpredictable," but pointed to the company's diverse customer base and strategic initiatives as mitigating factors.

Final Takeaway

Fastenal closed 2025 with robust sales growth, expanding key account relationships, and a rapidly increasing digital footprint. Management expects double-digit net sales growth in 2026, fueled by ongoing investments in FMI Technology and digital solutions, while maintaining disciplined cost management and capital allocation. The company acknowledged ongoing margin pressures but expressed confidence in offsetting these through SG&A efficiencies and additional scale, positioning Fastenal for continued market share gains and operational momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Roche more than doubles investment in new North Carolina site
data.description:
• Genentech, a unit of Swiss drugmaker Roche (RHHBF) (RHHBY), announced on Tuesday it plans to more than double its planned investment in a new North Carolina manufacturing facility where construction began in August.
  • Compared to its initial investment of $700M, the company will therefore make nearly a $2B commitment to the site to increase production volume and capacity within the facility, adding 100 new jobs to the North Carolina economy.
  • Located in Holly Springs, NC, the site will be Roche’s (RHHBY) first East Coast manufacturing facility, the pharma giant said. Once operational in 2029, the company plans to use it to produce treatments for metabolic diseases, such as obesity.
  • The investment will be part of Roche’s (RHHBF) pledge to spend $50B to boost its footprint in the U.S., where it currently operates 13 manufacturing and 15 R&D sites.

data.title: 3M flags tariff risk tied to Europe dispute; shares fall
data.description:
3M (MMM) on Tuesday warned that escalating trade tensions between the U.S. and Europe could pressure earnings in 2026, as the company assesses potential tariff exposure linked to a White House dispute involving Greenland.

The diversified industrial manufacturer ships roughly $700 million of products to Europe each year and imports about $250 million back into the U.S., according to Chairman and Chief Executive William Brown. Based on proposed tariff levels, Brown said the financial impact could become material. “If you just run the numbers at 10% and then expand up to 25%, you can get to something in the order of $60 to $70 million,” he said.

Shares of 3M (MMM) fell 8% to about $154.35 in midday trading on Tuesday.

The company said it expects revenue growth to accelerate in 2026 following a modest sales improvement in the fourth quarter, though its current outlook does not assume additional tariffs. Under the proposed policy, duties of 10% could be imposed early in the year and potentially rise to 25% by midyear. Brown said that scenario could translate into a $30 million to $40 million earnings impact in 2026.

3M (MMM) forecast adjusted sales growth of about 4% in 2026, up from $24.3 billion in 2025. The company projected adjusted earnings a share of $8.50 to $8.70, compared with $8.06 last year.

In the fourth quarter, net income fell to $577 million, or $1.07 a share, from $728 million, or $1.33 a share, a year earlier. Results were weighed down by litigation-related costs that reduced earnings by 56 cents a share. On an adjusted basis, earnings of $1.83 a share beat Wall Street expectations by three cents.

Quarterly revenue rose 2.1% to $6.1 billion, topping consensus estimates. However, margins emerged as a key concern.

Analyst Christopher Snyder of Morgan Stanley said margins were the primary downside surprise, citing a 360-basis-point sequential decline that exceeded typical seasonal patterns. He pointed to weakness in the consumer segment, where operating income came in 12% below expectations and margins slipped to 18%, a trend he said could pose near-term risk heading into the first quarter.

data.title: Estée Lauder faces a lawsuit over alleged tech secrets theft
data.description:
Beauty tech startup Nomi Beauty has filed a lawsuit against Estée Lauder (EL) in federal court in New York City. In the complaint, Nomi Beauty  alleged that the cosmetic product giant stole its hotel‑focused technology and trade secrets and effectively drove the startup out of business.

Lawyers for Nomi said the New York-based company shared proprietary methods as parts of agreements inked in 2018 and 2020 for figuring out what products customers truly prefer rather than what they say they want. Estée Lauder (EL) is accused of then terminating those contracts and launching its own competing hotel and travel‑retail programs in markets including China, Costa Rica, Malaysia, the U.K., and the U.S., using similar concepts and know‑how. Nomi claims Estée Lauder (EL) generated “literally billions” in new revenue while modernizing its sales channels and reducing dependence on brick‑and‑mortar stores. At the same time, Estée Lauder (EL) was accused of starving Nomi’s hotel partners of product supply and undermining its business, contributing to its collapse.

Nomi is seeking unspecified compensatory, punitive, and treble damages for trade‑secret theft and related misconduct. Estée Lauder (EL) has not yet provided a detailed public response to the allegations in the lawsuit.

Nomi Beauty's stated mission is to bring the best of what consumers love about Amazon, Sephora, and Duty Free America into the $30B+ travel beauty retail market.
